records.ety: [OE. soduen, OF. sosduire to seduce, L. subtus below (fr. sub under) + ducere to lead. See Duke, and cf. Subduct.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Subdued (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Subduing.]

defs: To bring under; to conquer by force or the exertion of superior power, and bring into permanent subjection; to reduce under dominion; to vanquish.
quotes: I will subdue all thine enemies.

defs: To overpower so as to disable from further resistance; to crush.
quotes: Nothing could have subdued nature To such a lowness, but his unkind daughters.
If aught . . . were worthy to subdue The soul of man.

defs: To destroy the force of; to overcome; as, medicines subdue a fever.
To render submissive; to bring under command; to reduce to mildness or obedience; to tame; as, to subdue a stubborn child; to subdue the temper or passions.
To overcome, as by persuasion or other mild means; as, to subdue opposition by argument or entreaties.
To reduce to tenderness; to melt; to soften; as, to subdue ferocity by tears.
To make mellow; to break, as land; also, to destroy, as weeds.
To reduce the intensity or degree of; to tone down; to soften; as, to subdue the brilliancy of colors.
